ocr.text: Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) infection is a significant public health concern, given its association with various gastrointestinal
diseases, including gastritis, peptic ulcers, and gastric cancer. Understanding the factors influencing H. pylori serology is crucial
for developing effective prevention and treatment strategies. The data presented in the accompanying table elucidate the
relationship between specific risk factors and H. pylori seropositivity, highlighting both crude and adjusted odds ratios (OR)
alongside their corresponding confidence intervals (CI). Notably, the column labeled "Crude OR (95% CI)" provides an initial
assessment of the association, while the "OR (95% CI)*" offers a more refined analysis that accounts for potential confounders.
These metrics allow for a comprehensive evaluation of the impact of various factors on H.
Factor  Status  Crude OR (95% CI)  OR (95% CI)*
H. pylori serology  Negative  1.00 (reference)  1.00 (reference)
Smoking  Never smoker  1.00 (reference)  1.00 (reference)
Mm-MAST** category  Low  1.00 (reference)  1.00 (reference)
γ-glutamyle transferase-tertiles (μkat/l)  1 (< 0.35)  1.00 (reference)  1.00 (reference)
pylori status, facilitating a deeper understanding of its epidemiology. For instance, the reference group, denoted by a status of
"Negative," serves as a baseline for comparison against other factors that may influence seropositivity. This nuanced
examination of H. pylori serology contributes valuable insights that can inform clinical practice and public health interventions
aimed at reducing the prevalence and associated morbidity of this infection.
